a: Paul announces the gospel as God's power for salvation, in which the righteousness of God is revealed (1:16-17). He then indicts the whole human race: Gentiles suppress the truth and stand under wrath (1), self-righteous moralists and Jews are equally guilty (2), and Scripture concludes that all have sinned and fall short of God's glory (3). The law silences every mouth, so that no one is justified by works—setting up justification by faith in Christ apart from the law.
summary: Universal guilt: Gentiles, moralists, and Jews alike are under sin so that no one is justified by works of the law.

a: Paul proves justification by faith from Scripture: Abraham was counted righteous by believing God, not by works or circumcision, so the promise rests on grace (4). The justified now have peace with God, access into grace, and hope that does not disappoint (5:1-11). Adam's one trespass brought condemnation to all, but Christ's one act of obedience brings justification and the reign of grace to many.
summary: Justification by faith proved from Abraham, issuing in peace with God and the reign of grace through Christ the second Adam.

a: Grace does not license sin: united to Christ's death and resurrection, believers are dead to sin and called to walk in newness of life, slaves no longer to sin but to righteousness (6). The law is holy but powerless against indwelling sin, exposing the believer's ongoing struggle (7). Chapter 8 crowns the argument: no condemnation in Christ, life in the Spirit, adoption, and the unbreakable golden chain securing the elect, from whom nothing can separate God's love.
summary: Sanctification through union with Christ and life in the Spirit, climaxing in no condemnation and assurance that nothing can separate us from God's love.

a: Paul defends God's faithfulness given Israel's unbelief. God's saving purpose rests on sovereign election, not human will or works, and the potter has the right over the clay (9). Israel stumbled by pursuing righteousness by works rather than the righteousness of faith that is near in the gospel (10). Yet God has not rejected His people: a remnant remains by grace, Gentiles are grafted in, and in the end God will have mercy on all, prompting Paul's doxology.
summary: God's sovereign election and faithfulness to Israel: a remnant by grace, Gentiles grafted in, and mercy that vindicates God's purpose.

a: The doctrinal indicatives ground the ethical imperatives: present your bodies as a living sacrifice, serve with spiritual gifts, and overcome evil with good (12). Believers submit to governing authorities and fulfill the law by love (13). The strong and weak in faith welcome one another over disputable matters for the sake of unity (14–15). Paul closes with his mission plans, greetings to fellow workers, and a final doxology to the only wise God.
summary: Christian living flowing from grace: self-sacrifice, love, submission to authorities, and unity between the strong and weak.

a: Paul confronts factions that boasted in human leaders and worldly wisdom. He preaches Christ crucified as the wisdom and power of God, foolishness to the perishing but salvation to the called (1). The Spirit, not eloquence, reveals God's wisdom (2), and leaders are mere servants and stewards through whom God gives the growth (3). Paul urges the puffed-up Corinthians to imitate his cruciform humility rather than their arrogance (4).
summary: Against divisions: the wisdom of the cross overturns human boasting, and ministers are only servants through whom God gives the growth.

a: Paul addresses moral and practical disorders. He commands church discipline of an incestuous man and warns against tolerating sin (5), rebukes lawsuits between believers and sexual immorality, since the body is a temple of the Spirit (6). He gives counsel on marriage and singleness (7), then on food offered to idols: knowledge must yield to love, and the strong must not wound weak consciences (8–10). The Lord's Supper and Israel's example warn against idolatry; all is to be done to God's glory.
summary: Correcting immorality, lawsuits, and idol-food disputes, urging discipline, purity, and love that limits Christian liberty.

a: Paul orders public worship. He addresses head coverings and rebukes abuses at the Lord's Supper, calling for self-examination since it proclaims the Lord's death (11). Spiritual gifts are diverse but given by one Spirit for the common good, forming one body (12). Without love, all gifts are nothing—love is the more excellent and abiding way (13). Prophecy is preferred over tongues because it edifies, and worship must be orderly, for God is not a God of confusion (14).
summary: Ordering worship: the Lord's Supper, diverse gifts in one body, the supremacy of love, and orderly edifying assembly.

a: Paul defends the resurrection as essential to the gospel: Christ died, was buried, and rose according to the Scriptures, witnessed by many. If Christ is not raised, faith is futile, but in fact He is the firstfruits guaranteeing believers' resurrection in imperishable bodies, swallowing death in victory (15). He closes with instructions for the collection for the saints, travel plans, and final greetings and exhortations to stand firm in faith and love (16).
summary: The resurrection of Christ and believers as the gospel's foundation, with closing instructions on the collection and final greetings.

a: Paul opens by blessing the God of all comfort who sustains in affliction (1) and explains his change of travel plans and the painful letter as motivated by love (2). He glories in the ministry of the new covenant, surpassing Moses' fading glory, carried in jars of clay so the power is God's (3–4). Reconciled to God in Christ who became sin for us, believers are new creations and Christ's ambassadors (5). He appeals for open hearts and rejoices at their repentance (6–7).
summary: The glory of new-covenant ministry carried in jars of clay, grounded in the message of reconciliation through Christ.

a: Paul organizes the collection for the poor saints in Jerusalem. He commends the Macedonians' overflowing generosity in their poverty and points to Christ, who though rich became poor to make us rich (8). He urges cheerful, willing giving, not under compulsion, promising that God loves a cheerful giver and supplies abundantly so that generosity overflows in thanksgiving to God (9).
summary: The Jerusalem collection: grace-motivated, cheerful generosity modeled on Christ, who became poor to make us rich.

a: Paul defends his apostleship against the 'super-apostles' who boasted in worldly credentials. He wields weapons of divine power and refuses to commend himself by human standards (10). Compelled to boast as a fool, he recounts his sufferings, labors, and the thorn in the flesh, learning that God's grace is sufficient and power is perfected in weakness (11–12). He warns the church to examine themselves, test their faith, and aim for restoration before his coming (13).
summary: Paul's defense of his apostleship, boasting in weakness so Christ's power may rest on him, with a call to self-examination.

a: Paul defends the gospel and his apostolic authority against the Judaizers. He pronounces a curse on any who preach a different gospel and insists his message came by revelation from Christ, not from men (1). He recounts confirming his gospel with the Jerusalem leaders and confronting Peter for withdrawing from Gentiles, asserting the core truth: a person is justified by faith in Christ, not by works of the law (2).
summary: Paul defends his gospel and authority, confronting Peter and affirming justification by faith, not works of the law.

a: Paul argues justification by faith from Scripture and experience. The Galatians received the Spirit by hearing with faith, and Abraham was justified by faith, so the blessing comes through faith, not law (3). The law was a temporary guardian until Christ, who redeemed those under the law's curse. Now believers are sons and heirs through faith, no longer slaves, having received the Spirit of adoption crying 'Abba, Father' (4).
summary: Justification by faith proved from Abraham and the Spirit, making believers sons and heirs rather than slaves under the law.

a: Paul applies gospel freedom. Christ has set believers free, so they must not submit again to the yoke of the law; yet freedom is not license but love serving one's neighbor (5:1-15). Those led by the Spirit walk in His fruit and crucify the flesh, rather than indulging its works. Believers bear one another's burdens, do good to all, and boast only in the cross, by which the world is crucified to them, for what counts is the new creation (6).
summary: Gospel freedom lived out: walking by the Spirit in love, bearing one another's burdens, and boasting only in Christ's cross.

a: The doctrinal half unfolds the believer's riches in Christ. Paul blesses God for election, predestination to adoption, redemption, and the sealing of the Spirit, then prays for the church to know its hope and Christ's power (1). Once dead in sin, we are saved by grace through faith, not works, and Jew and Gentile are reconciled into one new humanity (2). Paul reveals the mystery of the gospel and prays for the church to grasp Christ's surpassing love (3).
summary: The believer's riches in Christ: election and adoption, salvation by grace through faith, and Jew and Gentile made one in Christ.

a: The practical half calls believers to a worthy walk. Paul urges unity in the one body, with Christ's gifts equipping the saints to maturity, and a putting off of the old self for the new (4). They are to walk in love, light, and wisdom, imitating God, with instructions for Spirit-filled worship, marriage, children, and servants (5–6:9). Finally, they must stand against spiritual forces by putting on the whole armor of God and praying in the Spirit (6:10-20).
summary: The worthy walk: unity and maturity in the body, lives of love and light in households, and standing firm in the armor of God.

a: Paul gives thanks for the Thessalonians' faith, love, and hope, recalling how they turned from idols to serve God and wait for His Son (1). He defends his sincere, gentle ministry among them, like a nursing mother and a father, preaching the gospel without greed or flattery (2). Anxious for their faith under affliction, he sent Timothy and rejoices at the good report, praying that their love would increase and that God would establish their hearts (3).
summary: Thanksgiving for the Thessalonians' faith and Paul's tender, sincere ministry and ongoing care amid their afflictions.

a: Paul exhorts the church to holiness, sexual purity, brotherly love, and quiet diligent work (4:1-12). He comforts them about believers who have died: at Christ's return the dead in Christ will rise first, and the living will be caught up together to meet the Lord, so they grieve with hope (4:13-18). The day of the Lord comes like a thief, so they must stay awake and sober, encourage one another, and live in readiness, kept blameless until Christ comes (5).
summary: A call to holiness and the hope of Christ's return, when the dead in Christ rise and believers are gathered to the Lord.

a: Paul charges Timothy to confront false teachers who misuse the law, upholding the gospel that saves sinners, of whom Paul calls himself the foremost (1). He gives instructions for public worship, urging prayer for all people since God desires all kinds to be saved through the one mediator Christ (2). He then lays out the qualifications for overseers and deacons—men of proven character, sound doctrine, and a well-ordered household—so the church may conduct itself rightly (3).
summary: Guarding the gospel against false teaching, ordering public prayer, and setting qualifications for overseers and deacons.

a: Paul warns of coming apostasy and urges Timothy to train himself for godliness and to set an example in his teaching and conduct (4). He gives pastoral instructions on honoring various groups—widows, elders worthy of double honor, and slaves (5–6:2). He exposes the false teachers' greed, teaching that godliness with contentment is great gain and that the love of money is a root of evil, charging Timothy to flee it, fight the good fight, and guard the deposit (6).
summary: Pursuing godliness and good order in caring for widows and elders, with a warning against the love of money.
